meaning: 'soil (tr.), dirty, profane, defile, dishonor, contami- nate, spill sperm, (Y) be dirty'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: PbH. s`b (pîrel) 'render unclean', Syr. sayyeb, Aram. s`yeb 'be unclean', Md. sub 'pollute, defile'. The root is related to Ar. śâba (śwb) 'mix, adulterate' (also ta`vaśśaba 'be mixed').

SEMITIC: Heb. s`ôn 'shoe' (according to Zimmern 38 from Akk. šênu, also Blau 116; not so Kaufman 30, n. 1; Broekelmann 1928:454 considers Heb. s`ôn an Aramaic loanword), Aram. s`ênâ, Syr. s`â- nâ, Md. s`ona, Ug. m`yn (< Akkadian, Dietrich 299). ETHIOPIC: Te. s`on 'shoe', Tna. s`ani, Har. aśin, Gur. (Zway) aśnay, probably passed into Cushitic. Had. aśona. Reinisch 1902:329 compares Som. s`an 'leather sole'.

meaning: 'speak well, speak clearly, (T) be prompt, (Gr.132) answer promptly, successful, prosper'
origin_explanation: Dillmann 254 derives it from (ሤ`አ`ሰ`አ) 'answer' > ሤ`ሰ`አ (with loss of w) > reduplicated ሤ`ሰ`አ.

meaning: 'tinder, kindling mate- rial (such as dung, excrement), firebrand, wick'
origin_explanation: Dillmann 256 derives it from śaw`a (see below) 'sacrifice > burn'.

meaning: 'form, shape, fashion, carve, depict, portray, paint'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ug. m-ś-lt-m 'two figures' (Gordon 493). ETHIOPIC: Te. śâ`ala 'paint, depict, portray', Tna. śâ`ala, Amh. salâ; for beta śâ`al, cp. Tna. sa`li Kham. sil 'painting, picture'.

meaning: 'grow green, become green, be covered with vege- tables'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. śa`ar 'vegetation, trees'. There is a possible connection with ś`r, ś`r 'barley', for which see ś`rnây, below. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sa`r 'grass, hay', Te. sa`ar, Gur. sa`ar, Har. sa`ar, Amh.Gaf. sar, Arg. sir.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. śarâ 'heal', Gaf. sarâ; seems to be related to śaraya I (see below).

meaning: 'winnow, scatter, (T.K.M) separate, (T.K.Y) make dry, make disap- pear'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. śarâ (śry) 'disperse' related to śar`a, and śara (śyr) 'spread, disperse'. The comparison with Akk. serû, "etwa 'niederdrücken, unter- drücken'", Syr. sarâ 'attack', suggested by von Soden 1038, is unlikely. See also s`ry. ETHIOPIC: For G. mas`e, cp. Tna. mas`e, mas`e 'pitchfork', Amh. mas`e (from mas`e with insertion of n), Gur. (Soddo) mans`e (from Amharic); denomi- native Amh. männaśśâ, mässänä (with metathesis) 'winnow'. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. mas`a 'pitchfork'. See also mśry.
SEMITIC: Ar. śabba 'cut, pierce', PbH. śibbeb, Jud.- Aram. śibbâ 'splinter', Heb. śabāb-im 'splinters' (?), also in Cushitic: Bil. sab 'pierce, sting', Qua.Aw. Kham. sib, sib.

SEMITIC: śbh 'fat meat, corpulence'; perhaps also PbH. śabah 'grow in value', Syr. śabah 'im- prove, rise in value'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sabhe 'be fat', Te. sabha, Har. sab- ha, Amh.Gur. sabba. The comparison with Heb. śab`a 'be sated with food', Ar. śab`a, suggested by Armbruster 563, is unlikely. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa.Af. sebäh, subäh 'fat', Som. subag.

meaning: 'intertwine, put rush or leaves around something, be fenced in, be encircled by leaves or ivy'; śabśab 'trellis, basket made of intertwined rush, granary made of leaves or rush, (M) small room near the main house where the newly-married stay until their own house is built'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: for the meaning 'intertwine', cp. Ar. śabaḥ 'rope', Heb. śābaḥ 'turn around', Ug. śbb 'turn', Akk. śibbu 'girdle'. ETHIOPIC: Te. śabśab 'roofed anteroom', Tna. śāb- śāb 'small structure near the parents' house where the newly-married stay', Amh. śabśab 'lean-to, floor space protected from the weather by the edge of an overhanging roof', Gur. śabśab 'extension of roof'. For the meaning 'intertwine', cp. Amh. śābśābā 'plait', also Te. śābiḥ 'cord, thread', śābābā 'plait', Tna. Amh. śabāb 'cord or leather strap tied around the lower jaw of a horse' (perhaps, however, from the root śbb 'tie with a cord', rather than 'twist'). See also śabata.
see śyḅ.

meaning: (L.T.M) śabata (M) 'eat, have dinner'; (it is strange that Y gives śabata 'give a morsel', but śabata 'take a morsel'); 'ašbata, (T) 'aśbaṭa 'offer food, receive some- one for dinner, provide for, offer a feast'; śābāṭ, śabāṭ 'cooked food, dish of food, main meal, (Gr.131) feast, (Lt) maintenance'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. (ta)śābbaṭa 'consume', Tna. śārbāṭā, śārbāṭā (with an augmented r) 'taste a little bit', Har. subāṭ 'midday meal', Gur. (tā)śrabbaṭā (with augmented r) 'start to eat after taking an anthelmin- tic'; also in Cushitic; Qab. (tā)śrabbačo', same as Gurage. Or. subāṭa 'meal'.
gives the impression of being borrowed from Heb. śāde(h) 'field'.
From Heb. śadday; note that the Septuagint likewise renders śadday by 'omnipotent'.

meaning: 'flesh, meat, the flesh as opposed to the spirit, body, corpse'.
origin_explanation: (hafrata śagā 'genitalia'); (za-śagā 'of flesh, carnal, sensual'); (śagā-hu wa-damu 'Eucharist', lit. 'his flesh and his blood'); śagāwī, śagāy 'carnal, fleshy, corporeal, temporal'; (azmād śagāwāyān 'blood relatives'). ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh.Gur. śagā 'flesh', from Itic. Bil. stxa, Qem.Kham. siya (for more details on Cushitic, see Cerulli 1938:218, under sarā, and Dol- gopolsky 99-100). The comparison with Ar. śa- gawgā 'bulky', Heb. śāgā(h), śāgā (śgy 'grow great', suggested by Dillmann 267 (so also Koehler 914), is unlikely. See also śgw.

meaning: (K*) 'take a step, amble, (K*) cross, go across'.
origin_explanation: 'aśaggara, 'aśaggara, causative; also 'in- sert by skipping a day or a month in the count of years or holidays'; taśaggara (K*) 'cross over, step over'; śag(g)āri, śaggār (K*Gr.137) 'who am- bles, who takes a step'; śaggur (Gr) 'one who takes a step (toward), śagur 'intercalation, insertion'; śaggāre (Gr.137) 'accelerated steps'; śagrāt (K*) 'taking steps, ambling, crossing over'; śāgrāwī 'inserted'; 'aśgāri 'who inserts, (K*) who ambles, who makes cross'; maśgāri, maśgār (K*) 'who makes amble, who makes cross'; maśgār (pl. maśāgār) 'step, means of crossing over, means of taking a step'. SEMITIC: Pbh. śāgar 'run'; Schulthess 75, n. 33 compares Ar. śagara, rendered by him 'run'. While the original meaning of śagara is 'raise the leg' and not 'run', the comparison is still valid. Praetorius 1890:372 compares Ar. (ġsr), iġtasara 'cross', nominative from ġisr 'bridge'. ETHIOPIC: Te. śāgra 'run quickly', Tna.Amh. śā- gārā 'amble', Amh. (a)śaggārā 'do something every other day, skip a day (of a holiday)', Har. śāgāra 'amble', Gur. śaggara passed into Cushitic; Had. śāgārā-kko, Mo. śāggari (ye), Bil. śagar 'go out'. Note that Dillmann 266 compares the Geez root with Amh. (tā)śaggārā 'cross'.

meaning: (K*Gr) 'catch in a net, en- snare, entangle, (K*) bar a gate with a piece of wood'.
origin_explanation: 'aśaggara, 'aśaggara, causative; also 'cast nets, catch in a net, ensnare, entangle, fish'; taśaggara 'be captured, be ensnared, be caught in a net, get entangled'; śagāri, act. part. of śaggara; śagur (K*Gr.138) 'ensnared, captured, (Lt) spread (snare)'; śagrāt, verbal noun of śaggara; 'aśgāro 'who casts nets, fisherman, hunter'; 'aśgāro, 'aśgarot (Lt) 'hunting, fishing'; maśaggār 'fisherman, who lies in ambush'; (maśaggara 'aśā 'fisherman'); maśgart, pl. maśāgār, maśāgārt, (T) maśgar 'snare, trap, fishhook, net, network, trellis, fetters, ambush'. the basic meaning is perhaps that of interlacing things for a net, developed into 'catch with a net', for which cp. Ar. śaġara 'be intricate, be intricate, twined'. For the meaning 'interlace', Praetorius 1890:371 compares the root śrg-srg (with metathe- sis) represented in Heb. śrg (pu'al) 'be intertwined', Syr. śrag 'intertwine, entangle', Aram. śarīgā 'fence, trellis', Ar. śarīġa 'that which is woven of palm leaves (door, cage)'. On the other hand it is not impossible to consider the basic meaning 'close, enclose > catch', and in this case the root śgr may be compared (so also Dillmann 266) with Heb. śāgar 'shut up, close', Aram.-Syr. śagar, Ug.Md. Ph. sgr, Akk. śigaru, śigaru 'clamp'. Zimmern 15 and von Soden 1230 suggest a Sumerian origin for Akk. śigaru. ETHIOPIC: Te. śaggara 'catch (birds in snares)', māśa- gar (pl.) 'bars of a gate', Amh. (a)śaggārā 'catch (fish) in a net', śaggārā 'bar the gate with a piece of wood', Tna. śāg'ārā, śāgārā, Amharic śaggār 'difficulty' compared, with reservations, by Marras- sini 68, is a variant of čəggar and does not belong here. See also sarġa, śaggara III.

śagar, pl. śagārāt (K.Gr.137) 'keeper of the instrument of torture, executioner, guard, sol- dier of the guard, garrison'; śagārāwī, śagārātwī 'pertaining to śagar'; the various meanings of śagārāt given above proba- bly go back to śaggara II 'ensnare > close > keep imprisoned > keep watch'. Tna. śāg'arī 'prison keeper', Amh. śaggārā 'be taught drill' is perhaps a denominative from śāgarāt 'soldiers' taken from Geez. Praetorius 1890:371 does not connect śagara III with śagara II. He suggests the possibility of its being the proper name of a military unit.

meaning: (Deut.21:20) 'be a reveler' (in the Septuagint), 'be a glutton, be lavish' (in the Hebrew text).
origin_explanation: (Deut.21:20) 'be a reveler' (in the Septuagint), 'be a glutton, be lavish' (in the Hebrew text).
see śaggara III.
see (śgw), śəggəw.

meaning: 'become flesh, assume a body, become incar- nate, (K*) become corpulent, become stout'; 'aśagwa, (K*) 'aśaggawa 'incarnate, make stout, make corpulent'; 'astaśaggawa (K*) 'make corpulent'; śəggəw (fem. śəggut) 'incarnated, (K*) stout, fleshy'; śəggəwə 'fleshy nature, incarnation, becoming stout, becoming corpulent'; təśgut 'incarnation, assuming of a body'; taśaggəwət 'incarnation'; maśəggəw, maśəggəw (K*) 'who incarnates'; maśəggəw 'place of incarnation'.
origin_explanation: the verb is a denominative from śagā, above.

meaning: (L.T) śāhbaba 'grow moldy, grow musty, get spoiled'; śāhbub, (T) śāhbub, (L) śāhbub 'moist, moldy, musty, spoiled'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 230 (followed by Stade 29) connects it with Ar. śaḥuba 'be gray'. ETHIOPIC: Te. (a)śāhbaba 'overgrow', as of a plant that overgrows a stone (Littmann-Höfner 171), śāḥābā 'kind of moss', śābā 'moss', Tna. śāḥābā 'become moldy', śāḥābā, śaḥba 'moss' (so also par- tially Wajnberg 1937:34); Amh. śabā 'green scum that floats on stagnant water'. The connection with Tna. śāḥābā 'lose one's strength', saḥmāmā 'be without energy', suggested by Wajnberg 1935: 21(72), is doubtful.

meaning: 'be gracious, have mercy, show oneself merciful, be compassionate, show oneself propitious, be lenient, show compassion, forgive, pardon, have pity, be forgiving'; täšahäla, causative; täšahäli (K*), part. of täšahäla; täšahül 'clement, merciful, pardoned, pitied'; täšahül 'grace, compassion, clemency, favor, mercy, pardon, kindness, gentleness'; täšahäli (Gr.121; should be täšahäli) 'merciful, forbearing'; täšahlot 'compassion'; mašahl 'mercy seat, propitiatory, baldachin'; mašahl 'mercy seat, propitiatory'; mašatašahl 'compassionate, merciful, gracious, lenient, easy to be entreated, forbearing';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sahula 'be smooth, be level', sāhala 'be indulgent, be forbearing', SAr. s_hl 'plain'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. šähäla 'be benign', Te. šähäla 'be merciful'. Amh. tä-šähäla 'be better, be improved' (to be separated from the root s_hl 'ask', as suggested by Praetorius 1879:74 and Guidi 1901:199, 201). Gur. sarà (with r < l) 'look well'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. ta-šahal 'have pity'.
an augmented šlw to be compared with Heb. šālā (šlw) 'be at ease', Syr. šālā (šlw), Ar. salā (slw) 'become content'.

meaning: 'moon, first day of the month'; 'ašhərt (pl. of šähr), 'ašhərt (L) 'small holidays'; šähra (T), šähra (Y) 'begin (month)'; denominative; šähräy (Lt) 'lunatic';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. šahr 'moon, new month', SAr. š_hr 'beginning of a month'. Soq. šéher 'moon'. Heb. šähärōnīm 'little moons (adornment)'. Aram. šahrā 'moon', Syr. šahrā, Md. šira.

meaning: 'laugh, laugh at, mock, scoff at, jest, smile at'; causative; täšahaqa (Lt) 'laugh together'; 'ašhaqa (K*Gr), act. part. of šähaqa; šähuq (K*) 'who laughs, who mocks'; šähäq, šähaq, šähäq 'laughter, mocker sion, laughingstock, scorn'; 'ašhäqi (Gr.123), act. part. of 'ašhaqa; mašhaqi 'who makes laugh, mocker, p mašhäq, məšhäq 'place of laughter, the
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. sāhaq, sāhaq 'laugh', Ar. dah shq, Ug. shq. For Aram. gəhak, dəhak, Sy Md. ahk (h_k), ghk, see Noeldeke 1875 Brockelmann 1928:113. On this root see an ner 1977:111ff, R.M. Voigt, WO 10 (1979), 109-110. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sāhaqa 'laugh', Te. sāhaqa šähaqa, Arg. sāhaqa, Amh. saqä, Gaf saqa by assimilation (s-q > s-q). Gur. daqä, more details on this root in various Ethiopic guages, see Leslau 1979:216, 556.

meaning: 'be insolent, be arrogant, scorn, despise, insult, offend, be ve (K.T.Y.M) meddle'; šähäsi (K*), act. participle; šähus 'scorner, insolent, arrogant, mischievous who despises, (T.Y) who meddles into (Gr.124) irrational, misanthrope, (K) sults'; šähsat 'insolence, disdain, arrogance, dence, blasphemy, vehemence';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. šahasa 'be elevated, be lofty', bi- 'speak ill of someone', Heb. šahas 'g dignity'. PBh. šähäs 'be prominent, be exalted, brag'. See on this root Rabin, EI 9 (1969), 155. ETHIOPIC: Amh. səhus (KBT) 'who meddles (from Geez)'.

meaning: 'fresh dung'; šähbo (T*) 'worm that lives in dung';
origin_explanation: see sabo.

meaning: 'be at ease, be relaxed, be calm, be tranquil, be at rest, be comfortable, have respite, be tolerable, be allevi at tolerable position, be bearable, be allevi ed'; šähața 'comfort, alleviate, put at ease, allow to rest, quiet' (V); 'ašhața, caus. of šähta; 'ašahata, caus. of šähața; täšahța, see šähta; šähați (K.Gr.131), act. part. of šähta; šähuț 'tolerable, bearable, who is at rest, relaxed, who is at ease, uplifted'; šähuța (L), adverb of šähuț; šäht, (T) šäht 'rest, comfort, relaxation, tranquility, alleviation, relief, opportunity, good occasion'; 'ašhäți, mašhäți (K*), mašhəț (Gr.95), part. of 'ašhața.
'thorn, thorn bush, spine (of hedgehog), sting'; šoka (K*) 'sting, become thorny' (denominative); šokäwi (K*) 'thorny';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. šawk 'thorn(s)', Aram. šawkā; related to škk (skk). Ar. šakka 'pierce', Heb. śēk in śikkīm 'thorns', sukka 'harpoon', Aram. sikkā 'thorn'. Akk. šakāku 'be pointed'; sk' in Soq. ska'ah 'thorn'. Šh. ško'at, Ar. šakā'a 'plant that has thorns'. ETHIOPIC: Te. šok 'thorn', Tna. 'əšok, Gaf. əših 'a (h < k), Har. usux, Gur. sox. See also sak'a, above.

SEMITIC: Heb. śākar 'hire', Aram. (Palm.) Ug. škr; Ar. šakiriyy 'hireling, servant' is a Persian loanword. Palache 71 connects the meanings 'reward, hire' with Ar. šakara 'thank'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. 'ašäkar 'servant' is connected to Cohen 1930:33 with the Semitic root for 'hire'. However, on the basis of Tna. haššär 'servant, soldier' and Te. 'askär, one is inclined to consider the noun as taken from Ar. 'askar, unless in the meaning of 'servant' *ašäkar is derived from the root 'hire', and in the meaning of 'soldier' it is taken from Arabic.

meaning: 'grind fine, crush, peel, husk'; 'ašälaqa (K*), causative; täšälaqa (K*) passive; šäluq (Lt) 'ground fine'; šälaqo (Lt) 'something ground fine';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. salāqa 'an 'peel off (flesh) (from the bone)' (Armbruster 414); cp. also Akk. sāläqu 'cut'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sālāqä 'grind fine', Amh. sālāqä 'grind fine', sālāqqäqä 'husk, shell, hull', Gur. sālāqä 'grind fine'. Har. silīqe-zo wāta'a 'become very thin' (lit. 'his thinness came out'); passed into Cushitic: Kam.Al. silläqo, Or. silläqe.

meaning: 'triple, treble, do for the third time, act thrice' (denominative); 'ašällaqa (K*), causative; täšällaqa 'become triple, become three'; täšaläsa 'be the Trinity'; šäläs 'three' (with fem. noun); šäläsi 'who believes in the three persons of Godhead'; šäläsa 'thirty'; (ba-šäläsa 'thirtyfold'); šälus 'after tomorrow, three (days), the third day of the week or month, Tuesday'; (šälusa [Lt] 'after tomorrow'); šäläs (masc.), šäläst (fem.) 'third'; (ba-šäläs 'for the third time'); šäls 'three (used in measures), a third part'; šälsä 'thrice, three times'; šälläse 'Trinity, triad, kind of qəne-poem having three lines'; šällus 'that consists of three, threefold, triple, triplet, triangular, Trinity'; šälast 'tierce (prayer), name of a melody' (Velat 239ff); šälastu 'three' (with masc. noun); used with suffix pronouns, e.g. šälastihomu 'the three of them'; šälsit 'third time, third section of the day'; šälasta 'on the third day, in the third place'; šälastä 'əd 'a third'; šälsannä (Gr) 'Trinity'; šäläsäwi, šäläsäy 'third' (masc.); mašalläs (K*), act. part. of šällaqa; mašaläs (pl. mašaläs) 'triplet, kind of measure'; mašlast, mašläst, mašläst 'triple, threefold, three times as much, third rank, third order'; (mašlasta 'into three parts, triply'); tašläs 'third floor, third loft, upper part (of a building), scaffold, (Lt) steps'; tašaläs 'triple'; tašaläs (T) 'raised platform';
Trinity, threefold, three times as many'; (täsläsät 'ad 'third part')
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. talāt 'three', SAr. s³lt, Tlt, Soq. šile, Heb. šālōš, Aram.-Syr. tālātā, Md. tlāta, Ug. tlt, Ph. šlš, Akk. šalāš. ETHIOPIC: Te. sälas 'three', Tna. säläste; with loss of l in Amh.Arg. sost, Gaf. sostā, Har. šīšti, šīšti.
from Ar. sulṭān.
probably from Ar. šam' 'wax, candle(s)'; also in Te. šamā', Tna. šām'i, säm'i, Amh.Arg.Gur. säm, Gaf. sāmā' in Cushitic: Som. šama. Sa. säm'e. Aw. s'emi.
probably reconstructed from the imperfect yäšmək 'from *šammaka.

meaning: 'gain dominion, have power, have authority, exercise authority, become master, reign, rule'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sulṭa 'gain power or mastery over, prevail', Heb. šālaṭ 'lord over, dominate', from Aram. šalaṭ 'rule', Syr. šǝlaṭ (Wagner 113, 114), Ug. šlyṭ 'powerful', Akk. šalaṭu 'have power over'. For G. ('a)šalaṭa 'have power over, give power', a secondary formation from sälṭan, see Noeldeke 1910:39. On the Aramaic origin of Akk. šalaṭu, see Kaufman 98.

meaning: 'de light in, be pleased, be well pleased, have a liking for, like, take pleasure in, accept favor able, agree, consent, approve, grant, be of good will, condescend, (L) be pleasing'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Yem.) sabar 'be suitable, be pro fit' (with alternance of labials m-b), Heb. šāpar 'be pleasing', Aram.-Syr. šǝpar (with alternance of la bials m-p). The comparison with Syr. sǝbar 'think' suggested by Pratorius, DLZ 1900, 1696 is un convincing. See on this root Rundgren 1961:370. ETHIOPIC: Te. sämra 'please', Tna. sämärä, Amh. sämmärä 'be pleasing'; passed into Cushitic: Sa. samir 'delight in'.

SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. rasama 'draw, trace' (with metathesis for its Aramaic origin, see Fraenkel 250), Heb. rāšam 'inscribe', Syr. rašam 'draw, lineate'. ETHIOPIC: from smr 'rule lines'. Te. mäsmär 'line, stroke', Tna. mäsmär 'ruler', Amh. mäsmär 'line'. See also mazmar.
ETHIOPIC: Tna.Gur. šämätä 'deal in grain', Amh. šämmätä, Arg. šemmätä; perhaps a denominative from šm-t, to be compared with Soq. šiom 'sell', Akk. šāmu 'buy'.

phonetics: šane, šäne (L) šanǝ, tenth Ethio pian month (8 June-7 Juli)
meaning: tenth Ethio pian month (8 June-7 Juli)
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. šane, Amh. šäne; also in Cushitic: Kam.Tem. sanä. Be. senay 'early fall'. Dillmann derives it from šny 'be pretty, be good'. Because of the spellings ፠ and ፠ and Conti Rossini 1923:376 seems to be inclined to consider šane the original form and derives it from the Semitic root tny. 'two'. However, šane is not the 2nd month of the year and its derivation from the root for 'two' is doubtful. Besides, it is ፠ that is found in older manuscripts (Getatchew Haile).

SEMITIC: Dillmann 250 compares it with Syr. sǝnaq 'be in need'; cp. also Md. snq (for which see Dietrich 300). While Brockelmann 1928-485, following Zimmern 47, derives the Syriac root from Akk. sanāqu 'be in need', von Soden 1022 considers Akk. sanāqu 'be in need' an Aramaic loanword; see also Kaufman 93. ETHIOPIC: Tna. Har. sənqi 'provisions for a journey', Te. sənq, sǝq (< sənq), Amh. sənq; passed into Cushitic: Or sənqi.
variant of šaṣṣar (see šaṣṣara).

meaning: 'be beautiful, be good, seem good, be well, be fine, be excellent, be fitting, be appropriate'; with suffix pronouns: 'appear good, be good for, please'

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sulṭān 'power', Heb. šilṭōn, Aram.-Syr. sulṭānā. For the root in various Semitic languages, see Wagner 114. Geez sälṭan seems to be derived from Aram.-Syr. sulṭānā (Noeldeke 1910:39) rather than to be considered a noun with the suffix -ān derived from šallaṭa (for which see above).

phonetics: saqq (T) 'san 'beauty, charm (of speech), grace, goodness, excellence, virtue'; 'asannayi (Gr), act. part. of 'asannaya; 'asannayot 'favor, benevolence'; masannay 'beautiful, fair, the best (of), the best part, good things, who acts well, who does good'; masannay, same meanings as masannay; also 'good place'; mastasannay, mastasannay 'who restores, who puts right, who readjusts, who embellishes';
meaning: 'san 'beauty, charm (of speech), grace, goodness, excellence, virtue'; 'asannayi (Gr), act. part. of 'asannaya; 'asannayot 'favor, benevolence'; masannay 'beautiful, fair, the best (of), the best part, good things, who acts well, who does good'; masannay, same meanings as masannay; also 'good place'; mastasannay, mastasannay 'who restores, who puts right, who readjusts, who embellishes';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sanā 'good' is considered by Noeldeke 1910:59 a transcription of G. sannay 'good' rather than a loanword. While Noeldeke 59, n. 2 hesitates to compare G. sannaya with Ar. sanā (snw) 'shine', sanā 'brilliance', since one would expect sanā, Eitan 10 and 28, n. 50 does compare it with Ar. sanā (snw), with some reservations, and with Heb. yāšūn-ne (") in Qoh. 8:1. Eitan 28, n. 50 also compares Heb. šānī 'scarlet' with the Geez root sny, and Heb. miśne of Is. 61:7 is rendered 'best' by Eitan in HUCA 12-13 (1937-38). Bittner 1909:32 compares Mh. mesnā 'fitness, efficiency', and in WZKM 30 (1917-8):12-14 he compares the Geez root with Soq. sīnī 'see'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sännayā 'be good, be beautiful', Te. sāna, Gur. sännayte 'kind of false banana-plant (probably from sännayt 'beautiful'). Amh. tā-sänanā 'become beautiful, good'. Note that Amh. tā-sänanā can be either a reduplicative stem of a non-attested *sännā 'be beautiful', or it can be a reduplicative stem of *sännā used as asännā or tāsännā in the composite verbs. This *sännā is identified by Praetorius 1879:237 with G. sännaya. The root probably passed into Cushitic: Som. san 'beauty, goodness'.

phonetics: saqsaqa (K*.Gr.129), saqsaqa (T) 'interlace, make a network, make lattice perforate, bore, run through'; tasaqsaqa (K*.Gr.129), passive; saqsuq (D.247 under saqq), saqsuq (T) 'formed by network, perforated'; saqsaq (pl. saqsaq) 'network, fence made of netting, grate, lattice, any kind of work made like a net, veil, railing, window, (T) balcony'; saqsaqe 'being formed with network';

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: For 'perforate', cp. Ar. šaqqa, šaqaqa 'split', Ar. (Syria) šaqšaq 'tear, split, crack'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. šaqšaqā 'detach, separate something adhering to a hard object', Te. šaqšaqā 'move bread from the pan', Amh. šaqšaqā, saqšaq 'balcony, window, lattice ing'; also in Cushitic: Bil. Kham. saq 'plait, intertwine'. See also saqsaqa.

phonetics: saqaya (T) saqaya 'vex, afflict, torment, torture, molest, plague, inflict suffering'; tasaqaya, passive; also 'suffer pain'; saqayi, act. part. of saqaya; saqay, pass. part. of saqaya; saqay 'torment, torture, pain, vexation, affliction, tribulation'; masqe 'instrument of torture, goad, stimulus, weaver's comb, shuttle of loom';
meaning: 'vex, afflict, torment, torture, molest, plague, inflict suffering'; tasaqaya, passive; also 'suffer pain'; saqayi, act. part. of saqaya; saqay, pass. part. of saqaya; saqay 'torment, torture, pain, vexation, affliction, tribulation'; masqe 'instrument of torture, goad, stimulus, weaver's comb, shuttle of loom';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. šaqiya 'be wretched, be in distress', šāqā (šqw) 'distress' (tr), Šh. šiqi 'be tired', šiqi 'troublesome, tiring'. phonetics: saqq (pl. saqqat, 'asqaq, 'asqaqat) 'sackcloth, covering of goat's hair'; saqqa (K*) 'cover with sackcloth' (denomina tive);
meaning: 'sackcloth, covering of goat's hair'; saqqa (K*) 'cover with sackcloth' (denomina tive);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Soq. šeqah 'coat', Heb. šaq 'sackcloth', Aram.-Syr. šaqqa, Akk. šaqqu. For G. saqq, see Zimmern 67, and Noeldeke 1910:39. For the Semitic origin of Gr. sakkos, see Masson 24-5. For the Hittite origin of this root, see Rabin 1964:153. ETHIOPIC: Tna. saqq, Amh. saqq.

phonetics: saqs (K*) 'A corner of the earth, a part of the earth'.
meaning: 'A corner of the earth, a part of the earth'.

phonetics: sarwa (yašrāw) 'set, set forth, set in order (the table), establish, establish order, arrange, institute, enact, prepare, ordain, decree, stipulate, legislate, promulgate, prescribe, make regulations, regulate, make a covenant, make a testament, administer (the Eucharist)';
meaning: 'set, set forth, set in order (the table), establish, establish order, arrange, institute, enact, prepare, ordain, decree, stipulate, legislate, promulgate, prescribe, make regulations, regulate, make a covenant, make a testament, administer (the Eucharist)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. šara'a 'prescribe', šarī'a 'law', SAr. šr' 'erect, construct'. Buhl 794, following others (see bibliography, ib.), starts from the basic meaning Ar. šara'a 'point directly' developing into 'stretch out', and compares Heb. šr' (hitpael) 'stretch out', Syr. sra' 'extend' (so also Brockelmann 1928:500) perhaps also Akk. šerū, "etwa 'flach hinlegen'" (which see von Soden 1220). ETHIOPIC: Tna. sār'e 'arrange, decree', Te. sār'e 'hold in order, appoint', Amh. särra 'decree, prescribe, arrange', Har. sāra 'arrange'; perhaps common with Cushitic: Or. sēra 'law', Sid. sēra, Som. sār 'place, put', sōr'at 'rule'. Cerulli 1951:501 (under šerō) considers Or. sēra a South-Ethiopian loanword.

SEMITIC: Noeldeke 1910:61 considers the root to be common to Geez and Ar. širā' 'canvas, sail'. Fraenkel 213-4 derives it from Heb. šārā 'stretch'. Sārā', as against Landberg 1901:513 who derives from the original meaning of Ar. šr 'be high'. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. šārā 'sail', Amh. šārā (also sārā from Geez), Har. šara 'canvas, tent (of canvas)' passed into Cushitic: Sa. sirā, Af. sirā.

ETHIOPIC: Te. säräba 'suck up' (e.g. donkey sucking water), Tna. šarābā 'drink', Amh. šarābā (from Geez), Amh. mašrāb 'trough in which water is kept' comes from Ar. mašrāb. See also sarapa I.
from Ar. sarrafa, or common with sarrafa (Littmann 1913:17).
from Ar. sarg 'ditch for watering camels'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. särragā (from Geez).

meaning: 'prosper, make prosper, bring success, make successful'; 'ašarrahā 'give relief'; tasarrahā, (T) tasarrahā 'prosper, succeed, be successful'; šarrāhi (KG), act. part. of sarraha; šarruh, (T) šarruh 'prosperous, successful'; šarrāhāt 'prosperity'; tašarrahō 'prosperity';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. šaraha 'expand, widen', SAr. šrh 'de liver, preserve, save', Heb. šārah 'grow luxuriantly'.

meaning: in ǝštorah 'be overjoyed, be ravished with joy'. The variety of forms such as šǝrǝh, šǝrr, šǝrǝhe does not justify the suggestion made by Rundgren 1961:367 that G. šǝr(ǝ)ha is a loanword.
origin_explanation: The variety of forms such as šǝrǝh, šǝrr, šǝrǝhe does not justify the suggestion made by Rundgren 1961:367 that G. šǝr(ǝ)ha is a loanword.

meaning: ሐርመ (T.Y.K.M) šarama ሐርመ (T) 'become a swamp, be deep, sink, flood'; šarama (T) 'dig deep'; tašarma 'lower oneself into an abyss or to the ground, fall into an abyss, be submerged in an abyss, enter into a swamp'; šarm (T) šǝrǝm (pl. ǝšǝrǝm) 'depth, abyss, flood, swamp';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. šarm 'canal, small bay, inlet, main body of water' (from šarama 'slit, cut'), SAr. šrm 'outlet' (?). ETHIOPIC: Te. šǝrm 'puddle, hole filled with water'.

meaning: = Amh. ǝčǝfrag 'low-growing bush that is used like a brush in washing large crocks'.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 260 derives it from *šǝrǝrnay and connects it with Semitic šǝrǝr, Heb. šǝrōrâ 'wheat', Ar. ša'ir; cp. also SAr šǝrǝr, Hars. šǝrǝr, Aram. šǝ'arṭâ, Ug. šr (but see below). ETHIOPIC: G. šǝrnay as well as Tna. šǝrnay 'wheat', Te. šǝrnay, šǝrnay, Arg. šǝrray, Amh. sǝnde, Gaf. sǝndä (for nd:rn, see Praetorius 1879:78, Cohen 1931-380), probably also Har. sǝrri 'thick bread made of wheat' and Gur. sǝre, sǝnne, sǝne' 'wheat' are to be considered Cushitic loanwords, as suggested, with reservations, by Praetorius 1890:24, n. 9 (under haṭṭaṭ), and by Armbruster 625 (contrary to Leslau 1979:559): Sa. sǝnrä, Af. sǝrray, Som. sǝrēn, Be. sǝrām, Bil. šǝnräy.

meaning: 'rise (sun, new moon), arise, shine forth, spring forth, begin (month, year)'; ǝšraqa 'make shine, make rise, celebrate the new moon by special hymns'; šarǝq (T) šarǝq 'rising (of sun, new moon), day of a new moon, beginning of a month, (Lt) the Orient'; (šarqa warh 'new moon'); šarǝq 'rising (of stars, moon), new moon, the Orient'; šarǝq, verbal noun of šaraqa; also 'appearance'; šarǝqawi 'eastern, pertaining to a new (warh šarǝqawi 'lunar month'); šarǝqawi 'eastern, pertaining to a rising star'; ǝšarǝqot (Velat 472-3 has ǝšǝrǝqot) 'au petit jour, et avant de poursuivre l'office; le prêtre qui vient de chanter l'Evangile, chante le qot, c'est-à-dire, donne, pour l'an du cours, la date exacte des Pâques et des grandes fêtes'; mǝšraqa 'east, the Orient'; mǝšrǝqawi (K) 'eastern';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. šaraqa 'rise (sun)', SAr. šrǝq 'rise (star)', Hars. šǝrǝq 'rise (sun)', Heb. of Ben-Sira 'wr (msr)y/q (Noeldeke, ZA 20[1900].96); connected with 'be red': Ar. šarīqa 'be red', Heb. šaroq 'red' (Koehler 932). ETHIOPIC: Te. šarqa 'rise (new moon, star), Tna. šarǝqâ, Amh. šǝrrǝqâ.

phonetics: šǝrǝq, in ǝšǝrǝqo or šarǝqo ሐረቆ (corrected by Dillmann 240 into sǝrǝqo)
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. šaraqa 'slit', Mh. šǝrǝq 'comb', šarǝq 'card (flax)', Aram.-Syr. šǝrǝq 'comb' (that is something used for plaiting).
transcription of Gk. sōrek, from Heb. sōreq.

meaning: 'be high, be tall'; šǝrǝr 'length, height, altitude'; šǝrǝrat (Lt), same;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Perhaps Ar. šarra 'spread in the sun', that is, 'extend, *lengthen'. ETHIOPIC: Also in Cushitic: Bil. šǝr 'be high', Be. šǝrǝr 'tall'.

meaning: 'lay a foundation, found, establish, build, construct'; ǝšrǝrǝra, causative; tašrǝrǝra (Gr.165) tašǝrǝra, passive of šǝrǝra; šǝrǝri, act. part. of šǝrǝra; also 'founder';
'founded, set, established, (Lt) creature'; šururǝre 'foundation, founding'; mašarǝrat, see above.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Soq. šrr 'attach', PbH. šrr (hif-il) 'establish', Aram. šōrer 'establish, strengthen, fix firmly', Syr. šǝrar 'fix firmly'. For the identification of G. šrr with G. šǝrǝw 'root', Heb. šōreš, see Praetorius 1890:35. ETHIOPIC: Tna. šǝrǝtä 'lay a foundation', denominative from šǝrǝr 'foundation', from *šrr, šrr.

meaning: šarada ሐረደ 'germinate, blossom, shoot forth, sprout, burgeon, bud, proceed (Holy Ghost from the Father), arise, (Lt) conceive, think out'; ǝšraša, causative; šarǝši, act. part. of šaraša; šarǝš 'that germinates, that blossoms'; šarǝš 'shoot, blossom, sprout, bud, young twig'; šarǝšat, verbal noun of šaraša; also 'procession (of the Holy Ghost)'; šarǝš (K*) 'proceeding (of the Holy Ghost from the Father)'; šǝrǝšǝnnâ 'procession'; ǝšrǝši, mǝšrǝši, participle of ǝšraša; mǝšrǝš 'that germinates'; mǝšrǝš 'place of the germination';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. šaraša 'start walking (young animal)', Heb. šǝraš 'swarm, team'. In the meaning of 'creep' (see P. Joüon, Biblica 21[1940].152-3), as also šǝraš 'creep', it can be semantically related to šaraša 'germinate', the basic meaning being that of change in place or growth, contrary to Conti 1978:35; cp. also modern Syr. šǝrǝš 'issue, descent'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. šǝrǝšâ 'sprout', Amh. šǝrräṭâ 'suffuse' (Leslau 1979:74), and perhaps also Amh. (ǝ)šǝrǝččâ 'spread (news)' (Armbruster 529), Gur. ǝšrǝččâ, even though the root of Amh. and Gur. is a quadriradical šrṣy.
Dillmann 245 and KWK 677 derive it from the root šǝrǝy 'weave' (see šaraya II). ETHIOPIC: Te. šarǝt 'spider', Tna. šarǝt, šǝrǝrǝt, šǝrǝrǝt, Amh. šǝrǝrǝt, Arg. ǝnšararǝt, South Arg.

meaning: Har. ǝšširǝrahti, Gur. šarǝt. Cerulli 1936:237 considers it a Cushitic loanword: Or. sa-rarit, Bil. šarǝro, Qem. šarǝri, Aw. šǝrǝnt; so also Brockelmann 1950:28.
origin_explanation: Cerulli 1936:237 considers it a Cushitic loanword: Or. sa-rarit, Bil. šarǝro, Qem. šarǝri, Aw. šǝrǝnt; so also Brockelmann 1950:28.

meaning: šarwe ሐረወ (T) šarwe ሐረወ 'beam of wood, log, trunk, weaver's beam';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Aram.-Syr. šǝrīṭā 'beam' (Dillmann 242); Ar. šarīya 'pillar' is considered by Fraenkel 11 an Aramaic loanword, contrary to Schulthess 23, n.2. ETHIOPIC: Amh. šǝrwe 'mast, weaver's roller', and perhaps Gur. šarǝt 'part of a loom'.

meaning: šǝrǝw ሐረወ 'sinew, tendon, nerve, muscle, basis, root, origin, stock, tribe, (T) belief' (that is, the basis); (šǝrǝw hǝrum 'sinew of the thigh', lit. 'the forbidden muscle'; it reflects the Heb. gid hannāše 'the sinew of the thigh-vein' which people are forbidden to eat, according to Gen. 32:32); šarawa (Y.K*) 'take root' (denominative); šarrawa (T) šarrawa 'extirpate, eradicate, exterminate, root out, destroy' (privative denominative); tašarrawa 'be rooted'; tašarrawa 'be eradicated'; šarrawi, act. part. of šarrawa; šarraw, fem. šarrut (K*) 'that which is eradicated, destroyed'; šarrawǝwe 'eradication, being eradicated, destruction, defeat'; mašrǝw (Gr.127) 'instrument for uprooting'; tašarrawǝwo (Lt) 'destruction';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. širr 'origin, source' > *root*, SAr. šrṣ 'foundation', h-š-rṣ, 'extirpate' (denominative), Soq. šǝrǝh 'origin, source', Heb. šōreš 'root' (from šr-šr), Syr. šǝrǝšâ, Ug. šrš, Akk. šuršu. For Heb. šōreš and others, see Rüžička 11. F. Schwally, ZDMG 52 (1898). 140 connects Heb. šōreš with Ar. šaras, širs 'thorny plant', ǝšras 'hard', the basic meaning being 'knotty, gnarled > root'. For Hebrew, cp. also šōr 'umbilical cord' (Appleyard 16). Ar. širš 'root' (Dozy) does not give the impression of being an original Arabic root. ETHIOPIC: Te. Amh.Gaf.Har.Gur. šǝr 'root', vein, sinew', Tna. šǝr, Sur. Gaf. šǝrǝt, Arg. sǝrǝd; also in

a plural of *šurrī, šurre; from Ar. sirwāl, pl. sarāwīl (for which see Dozy 1845:203-9). ETHIOPIC: Te. Tna. surre, Amh.Gaf. surrī. For Amharic, Armbruster 493 also gives a plural sarawər; in this case surrī, surre may be a reconstructed singu- lar. The noun also occurs in Cushitic: Or. surrī, Qab.Al. surrī-ta, Bil. surrī, Sa. surre.

meaning: 'be- witch, cast spells, poison, heal, cure, give medi- cine'
origin_explanation: SEMÎTIC: SAr. sry 'save, protect' (Müller 1983:283), Aram.-Syr. sarī (sry) 'have a bad odor', Ar. šary 'colocynth' (which has a bitter taste; Brockelmann 1928:499), Heb. šōrīyyā 'stinking, putrid' (Koehler 654). The bad odor comes from the poison. ETHIOPIC: Te. sara (sry) 'cure, treat medically', saray 'remedy, poison', Amh. saray 'poison, poi- sonous philter' (from Geez), šarāyyā 'to poison'. For 'medicine, poison', cp. also Amh. mādhannīt, Syr. sam, Md. sama 'drug, medicine, poison'. For Indo-European 'drug, poison', see Buck 311. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. sirā, zirā 'poison'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. šarāyyā 'weave'. See šāret, and šerāya II.

meaning: (T) 'dye, paint, dip, smear with blood, stain soil'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. siray 'red color'; perhaps Cushitic: Bil. sar 'be red', Qua. sār, zār, Aw. sār. See also sirā.

SEMÎTIC: Heb. śārā (śry) 'contend', Ar. šarīya 'angry', šarā (śry) 'do evil'. ETHIOPIC: Te. šīra 'plot' (n.), Tna. sera, šāra connected by Coulbeaux-Schreiber 220 with Tna. šarāyyā 'weave' (see šaraya II, above; cp. Tramer 'meaning 'weave' and 'plot'). Amh. gur. sera 'plot'. See also šaraya II.

meaning: 'costly garment, silk, pure linen, (T) red cloth'
origin_explanation: from Aram.-Syr. šīrā, šerā›ā, šīrāyā 'silken (Dillmann 263, Noeldeke 1910:40); also Ar. ra›ā. See also šīryon.

meaning: 'cross out a word by drawing a line, (MA) separate'
ETHIOPIC: Tna. sārāzā 'cross out', Amh. sarrāzā 'cross out, rule with lines the Greek letters serve as numbers'

origin_explanation: undoubtedly for šasāra; SEMÎTIC: Heb. śī›dār 'take delight in' (from śr), śā›d (śry) 'engage in sports, divert oneself'. ETHIOPIC: Te. (ta)šasṣa›a 'dance (women)', Tna. sasṣare 'passed into Cushitic: Bil. sasṣa. See soso.

meaning: 'be insatiable, be greedy, eat much, (Y) crave, be covetous'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. sasṣa›e 'be greedy', Amh. sasṣat 'voracity', Har. sisṣit ›āša 'be greedy', lit. 'make voracity'; denominative: Amh. Gur. sasṣātā 'be greedy'; passed into Cushitic: sasṣat 'voracious'. Ethiopic śs›, ss› goes back to s›s and is possibly to be connected with św. The root is also related to Te. (ta)sāwḥa 'have appetite, desire', Ar. sāḥa (śwh) 'desire', šahwa 'covetous- ness'.

meaning: 'be thin, be sparse, be far apart'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. sasṣahe, Amh. sasṣa.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. saṣun, sasṣon 'coffer', Amh. saṣon; Ar. šawāšīn (from *šāṣūna) is considered Noeldeke 1910:55 a loanword from Geez. See also Landberg 1942:2049.

meaning: 'split (wood), cut up, tear, cleave, lacer- ate'

origin_explanation: SEMÎTIC: Ar. šasara 'pierce', Soq. šīṣehar 'split' (n); related to Ar. šatara 'divide in two', with metathe- sis in Ar. šarata 'split', Heb. šārat 'make incisions', Aram. šarat, Akk. šarāṭu. ETHIOPIC: Te. sāṭra 'split, crack', Tna. sāṭāra, Har. sēṭāra, Gur. šāṭārā 'split wood in half'; enlarged by n in Te. sanṣar, sānsar 'splinter', Tna. sanṣar, Amh. sāntārā 'split', Gur. sanāṭārā; Amh. santar, sanəṭar, ṭanṭar 'splinter'. See also sarwaṣa, šənṣar.
probably a misprint for šēṭ 'value', šēṭ-a 'in return for' (see šyṭ); or could it be for ›asēṭ 'compensation, salary'?

SEMÎTIC: Ar. šaṭara, šanṭara 'tear, split, slit', SAr. s.ṭr, Heb. šāṭar 'pull out', Syr. šaṭar, Md. str 'destroy', Akk. šuṭṭu- ru; related to štr. Ar. šaṭara 'divide in two', Soq. šəṭar 'cut, slit'. ETHIOPIC: Te. sāṭra 'comb' (v), Tna. sānṭārā 'comb' (v), māšāṭər 'comb' (n), Amh. sanāṭārā, šanāṭārā 'tear open', Gur. šarrāṭā (with metathesis) 'make decorative incisions on a pot'. For G. šaṭr 'line', cp. Ar. šaṭar 'lengthwise cut' (Kazimirski), and perhaps also saṭar, saṭar (with ṭ) 'line'.

meaning: 'cleave, split (tr., intr.), tear asunder, break through, (Lt) open a way'

phonetics: ṣataqa (ṣataqa māʾakalomu [Lt] 'he fell upon them', lit. 'he split their midst');
meaning: 'he fell upon them', lit. 'he split their midst';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Aram.-Syr. ṣədaq 'split (v.), PbH. ṣedeq 'split' (n.), Aram.-Syr. ṣədaq 'split' (v.), Md. ṣdq. An original stq > sdq by dissimilation of voicing (t-q > d-q). Barth 1893:38 considers sdq to be the original root. Praetorius 1890:25, note, compares G. stq with Ar. iš-ta-qqa 'split' (from šqq) with agglutinated t > t by assimilation. ETHIOPIC: Te. šattaqa 'split', Gur. šättäqa; with augmented n in Tna. ṣənāttāqā, Amh. ṣənāttāqā.

phonetics: ṣatuy (D.K) 'confused in mind, deprived of reason, who is bad-natured, (K*) who leans to one side';
meaning: 'confused in mind, deprived of reason, who is bad-natured, (K*) who leans to one side';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. ṣāṭā (ṣṭy) 'turn aside', Aram.-Syr. ṣāṭā (ṣṭy), Md. ṣṭā 'turn aside, deflect, lead related to Ar. ṣaṭṭa 'act unjustly'.

phonetics: ṣataya II ሠጠየ (L.M) ṣataya ሸጠየ 'tear, lacerate, rend, be terror-stricken, be dismayed';
meaning: 'tear, lacerate, rend, be terror-stricken, be dismayed';
origin_explanation: (L), tasatya (T) 'be panic-stricken' related to ṣataqa, above.

phonetics: ṣataya III ሠጠየ (M) 'love one another, fit together'
origin_explanation: (one would expect taṣāṭaya).

phonetics: ṣaṭata (yaṣṭət) ሠጠጠ (L.T.Y) ṣaṭaṭa ሸጠጠ 'tear apart, split, rend, cleave asunder, rip up, (T.Y) scrape';
meaning: 'tear apart, split, rend, cleave asunder, rip up, (T.Y) scrape';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Dat.Yem) ṣaṭṭa 'cut, split, tear', Akk. ṣaṭāṭu 'tear out' (?). ETHIOPIC: Te. ṣāṭṭa 'rend', ṣāṭṭa, ṣāṭṭaṭa 'tear', Tna. ṣāṭāṭā, ṣāṭāṭā 'break', Amh. ṣāṭṭaṭā 'tear noisily or quickly'; also in Cushitic: Be. šetit 'tear off'. See also ṣataya II.

phonetics: *ʾanṣoṭaṭa እግጾጠጠ (L.T) ʾanṣoṭaṭa እግጾጠጠ mm 'shiver, shudder, quiver, be afraid, be horror-struck, be alarmed, stand on end (hair), make the hair stand on end for a fight, frighten, terrify';
meaning: 'shiver, shudder, quiver, be afraid, be horror-struck, be alarmed, stand on end (hair), make the hair stand on end for a fight, frighten, terrify';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps to be identified with Syr. ṣaʿaṭ 'shudder', Md. ṣuʿ (ṣwʿ) 'hold in abomination'. See also ṣawṭaṭa.

phonetics: ṣaṭaya I (yaṣṭiʾ) ሠጠየ (T.Y) ṣaṭaya ሸጠየ 'drink, be drunk';

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Aram.-Syr. ṣəwaḥ 'sprout, spring up', Zimmern 70 derives it, with reservations, from šamāḥu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ṣāwḥe 'grow green'.

phonetics: ṣawwana ሠውነ (K) ṣona ሦነ (T) ṣawwana ሸውነ 'foam (v), dribble, drool';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. sāwwāna 'foam'. The comparison with Tna. ṣawan 'arrogant, proud' suggested by Coulbeaux-Schreiber 259 is unconvincing.

phonetics: ṣawr, maṣwar, maṣwart ሠውር, መሥወር, መሥወርት (pl. maṣāwər) 'funnel, (Lt) dry measure, container for oil, means of stirring the fire poker';
meaning: 'funnel, (Lt) dry measure, container for oil, means of stirring the fire poker';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Sar. m-ṣ-w-r-t 'irrigation channel'. Rabin SH 8 (1961). 394 compares it with Heb. maṣūrā 'hollow measure' (Koehler 570), and with Ar. maʿšār, mišwārā 'beehive' (perhaps because of its shape). In view of Sar. m-ṣ-w-r-t the Geez root is perhaps swr (Müller 1983:283).

phonetics: ṣawṭaṭa ሠውጠጠ (Lt) 'fall into perplexity or terror';
origin_explanation: see šṭṭ. ʾanṣoṭaṭa.

phonetics: ṣawaya (yaṣwəy) ሠወየ (L.T.Y) ṣawaya ሸወየ 'ripen (grain), mature (grain), form ears (corn)';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. sāwāyā 'ripen', Te. sāwet 'ear (of corn)', Amh. ašāt, ašet, Har. ašīta, asīta 'young grain'; denominatives: Te. sāwwāṭa 'form ears (corn)', Tna. sāwwāṭā, Amh. sāṭā; also in Cushitic: Sa. suwā 'grain that ripens', Qem. sāwi.

SEMITIC: Littmann 1913:17 connects the root with Heb. šōʾā 'desolation' even though the correspondence Geez ṣ : Heb. š is not normal; perhaps also to be connected with the various Semitic languages mentioned under sayʾa, above.

phonetics: ṣyb, ṣəba (yaṣīb) ጼብ (L.T.Y) ṣəba ጼብ 'turn grey (hair), turn white (hair), have grey hair, (MA) grow moldy';
meaning: 'turn grey (hair), turn white (hair), have grey hair, (MA) grow moldy';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. šāba (šyb) 'have white hair', Sh. šub (šyb) 'have grey hair', Heb. śāḇ (śyb) 'have grey hair', Aram.-Syr. śyḇ, Md. sāḇ, sīḇ, Ug. šbt 'whiteness of hair' (Caquot 173, note g), Akk. šību 'grey, old, old man'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ṣāyyəbā, ṣāyyəbā 'have grey hair', Te. ṣāyyəbā: 'grey hair', Tna.Amh.Gur. ṣābāt, Te. ṣibāt, Har. ṣibāt, Arg. ṣābād; denominatives from ṣābāt: Tna. ṣābāṭā 'have grey hair', Amh.Gur. ṣābāṭa. For denominatives formed with prefixed or suffixed morphemes, see Leslau, Muséon 75 (1962).139-175.

phonetics: ṣym, ṣəma (yaṣīm) ጼም 'set, set in order, put, place, appoint (to an office), ordain, charge, establish, designate, institute, promote, consecrate, (Y.M) hide (that is, "place something aside"), insert';
meaning: 'set, set in order, put, place, appoint (to an office), ordain, charge, establish, designate, institute, promote, consecrate, (Y.M) hide (that is, "place something aside"), insert';

phonetics: ṣwt, ṣoṭa (yaṣuṭ) ሠወጥ (T.Y) ṣawata ሸወጠ (T.Y) 'bring back, make turn back, return (tr), restore, turn back';
meaning: 'bring back, make turn back, return (tr), restore, turn back';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ṣāṭa (šwṭ) 'run to a goal', šawwaṭ 'make a long journey'. Heb. šāṭ (šwṭ) 'rove about', Aram. šwṭ, (polel) šōṭeṭ, Md. šāṭa 'wandering about'. The connection with Akk. šāṭu 'rebel' suggested by Blau 115 is not convincing. Note that Akk. šāṭu is rendered by von Soden 1205 either a šāṭu I 'ziehen, schleppen' or as šāṭu II 'gering achten missachten'. See also ʾasata.

SEMITIC: Ar. šäma 'put into, insert, introduce', SAR. s₂ym 'set up', Heb. śām 'insert, put, hide', Aram. śām, Syr. śām, Md. sum, Ph. śm 'place, set', Akk. šāmu 'determine'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. säyyämä 'appoint'; denominatives of *śäyum 'appointed' with palatalization of ś in Amh. šomä 'appoint to an office', Te. šäyäma. For 'hide', cp. Gur. šämä. For G. mäśyäm, cp. Amh. mäśyäm 'cupboard for storing' (from Geez). See also šymt.

meaning: (pl. säyyumän, šäyyami), pass. part. of šema; also 'appointee, official, governor, prefect, one who is in charge (of)';
'placement, putting, appointment to an office, ordination, jurisdiction, consecration, investiture, office, dignity, position, rank, hierarchy, charge, function';
'container, basket, basket serving as a measure';
'location where something is placed (e.g. barn, basket, office)';

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. säyyämä 'reveal (light that reveals objects formerly concealed by darkness)'.
denominative from šimat (*šaymat); see šym I, above.

SEMITIC: Ar. matāna (from ryn) 'bladder', Heb. *šyn 'urinate', Syr. tān (twn), Akk. sānu, Ug. tnt 'urine'. W. Müller (private communication) is of the opinion that in view of Ar. matāna (with r), the Geez root is syn rather than šyn. ETHIOPIC: Te. šena 'urinate', Tna. šenä, šänä, šänna, šännä.

SEMITIC: Ar. (Yem) šāt (šyt) 'sell', štāt 'buy (grain)', SAR. (Qataban) s₂yt 'trade', s₂yty 'buy, trade' connected with Heb. šāt (šwt) 'rove about' (so also Dillmann 264, following Gesenius), Ar. šawwata 'make a long journey'. For trading connected with moving about, cp. Heb. sāḥar 'go to and fro, trade'. The connection with Ar. šarata 'impose a condition' suggested by Praetorius 1890:290, is unlikely. For buying and selling expressed by the same root, cp. Ar. bā'a 'sell', ibtā'a 'buy', šarā (šry) 'sell, buy'. Rabin in Ben-Hayyim 483 connects the Geez root with Heb. šwt, šty 'be cunning, entangle oneself in falsehood'.

meaning: 'buy back, sell among themselves, trade, practice trade';
'pertaining to a marketplace, one who goes to market';
'detach, separate, remove, (K) show partiality (by making a distinction between persons), turn one's back, keep at a distance, draw away, prohibit';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. šadda 'separate', Soq. šeze 'detach' (whose form, however, is that of the causative-reflexive of ezi 'separate'). Praetorius, ZDMG 61 (1907).624 thinks that G. šäzäya is a modern South Arabian (Soqotri) loanword.
